text: Cal Newport calls it the "shutdown ritual" — a deliberate sequence of actions that convinces your brain the workday is truly over. The ritual matters because the brain's default mode network keeps rehearsing unresolved tasks during ostensible rest, a phenomenon psychologists call the Zeigarnik effect. Without a formal close, the prefrontal cortex continues scanning for incomplete items, bleeding cognitive resources into the evening. Newport's own ritual ends with the spoken phrase "shutdown complete" — not merely symbolic, but a conditioned cue that interrupts rumination. The specificity of the phrase, repeated identically each day, builds an associative anchor the nervous system learns to trust.
question: According to the passage, why does Newport use the exact same spoken phrase to end his shutdown ritual each day?

options.text: To signal to colleagues that he is no longer available
Because Newport believes verbal affirmations reduce cortisol levels
To build a conditioned associative anchor that the nervous system learns to trust
To satisfy a journaling habit he developed during graduate school
